summary.en: Once known as the 'Paris of the East,' this leafy district is an architectural marvel that blends traditional Chinese shikumen (stone-gate) houses with European design flair. It is characterized by well-preserved 1920s-1940s garden villas, cozy alleyways, and tree-lined streets, bustling with boutique shopping, small galleries, and a cosmopolitan nightlife.

Continuously inhabited since the 3rd century AD, this district is an incredible mix of ancient history and modern commercial power. It features historic residential pockets interspersed with towering skyscrapers and high-end shopping.

This area serves as the historic core and former colonial riverside. It is defined by its stately 1920s architecture—including Neoclassical, Art Deco, and Gothic styles—that once housed foreign banks and trading companies.

The historic walled Chinese area that existed long before the foreign concessions. Though the walls are gone, it preserves ancient wooden architecture, narrow bustling lanes, traditional gardens, and deep cultural roots.

Located north and east of downtown, this area was the former Japanese concession and a safe haven for Jewish refugees in the 1930s. It offers a unique mix of quiet residential life, deep literary history, and repurposed industrial spaces.

A hyper-modern, skyscraper-laden financial and commercial district on the east bank of the Huangpu River. Developed rapidly from farmland since the 1990s, it represents futuristic Shanghai and boasts some of the tallest buildings in the world.

description.en: A down-to-earth rock 'n' roll bar where locals and expats mingle over tasty booze.
An independent local brewery serving craft drinks and food.
An extremely popular restaurant known for its excellent Sichuan food served in classy surroundings.
A beloved local spot for MSG-free, classic Shanghainese dishes like red-braised pork. (Note: Not from sources; verify independently.)
Overhauled, picturesque shikumen alleyways that emphasize arts, crafts, and unique boutique shopping.
A historic street lined with well-preserved garden villas that once housed famous historical figures.
A park featuring formal French-style gardens where you will likely find locals playing wei qi (Go).

name.en: Shanghai Propaganda Poster and Art Centre

description.en: A hidden, private collection of uncensored Mao-era posters located inside a residential apartment building.

Housed in a converted power plant, this is China's first state-run contemporary art museum.
Located in a park at the north end of the Bund, offering historical context to the waterfront.

A classic, historic tea house situated in the middle of a traditional pond.

name.en: Temple of the Town God (Chenghuang Miao)

description.en: The spiritual heart of the Old City, dedicated to the city's protector deities.
A serene oasis dedicated to Confucius.
A quirky cafe nestled inside the historic abattoir. (Note: Not from sources; verify independently.)

A former abattoir converted into a creative hub with theaters, studios, and boutique shops.

name.en: Shanghai Jewish Refugees Museum (Ohel Moishe Synagogue)

description.en: Commemorates the thousands of Jewish refugees who fled Nazi Germany and settled in Shanghai.
A historic pedestrian street known for its literary heritage, old residences, and antique shops. (Note: Independently verifiable place in Hongkou.)
Located high up in the Ritz-Carlton, offering unmatched river views. (Note: Not from sources; verify independently.)
A famous Chinese independent specialty coffee roaster. (Note: Not from sources; verify independently.)
An internationally renowned spot famous for its xiaolongbao; while it operates multiple locations, its Pudong outpost is highly recommended for world-class dumplings.
An independent fine-dining experience with spectacular skyline views. (Note: Not from sources; verify independently.)
Currently the tallest building in China, featuring a massive spiraling architectural design.
A massive, iconic TV tower with distinctive spheres.
Located underneath the Pearl Tower, it offers a fascinating look into the city's past.
A network of pedestrian-friendly footbridges offering unobstructed views for photographing the towering skyscrapers of Pudong. (Note: Derived from context; verify independently.)

title.en: Green Space in the Concrete Jungle

prompt.en: Find a park or green space in Lujiazui and take a photo of nature surrounded by giant buildings.
Capture the dazzling neon lights and building illuminations of Pudong at night.
Take a photo of a massive logo of a bank or corporation on one of the skyscrapers.

Find a restaurant and photograph a beautifully presented dish, imagining it as a power lunch.
Frame a shot of another skyscraper by looking through a part of the Oriental Pearl Tower's structure from below.
